title: The link
checkLabels.t5: The address is not on show
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of making a link. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: closes with . Between them sits the word you click on.
hint3.not-closed: word — three parts: an opening with the address, the word, the closing.
why.no-link: There is no link with words in it. Either there is no <a>, or the tag is empty. An empty link is the worst kind — it clicks, but a blind person hears only the address, character by character.
hint2.no-link: There must be text between and . That is what a person sees and clicks.
hint3.no-link: the official site — the address is outside, the words are inside.
hint4.no-link: Wrap words from the sentence in <a> — "this one", for instance. The address goes in href.
why.no-href: The tag is there, but it does not say where it leads — or the href is empty. With no address, a link is a button that does nothing.
hint2.no-href: href is an attribute. It lives inside the opening tag, not between the tags.
hint3.no-href: the word — the address goes in quotes, inside the .
why.click-here: You wrote "click here". Take that word out of the sentence and read it alone: "here". Where? A blind person often skims ONLY the links on a page — their computer pulls them into a list. If they all say "here", they know where none of them lead.
hint2.click-here: Read your link text on its own, without the sentence around it. Can you tell where it goes?
hint3.click-here: "Click here for prices" → the link is "prices", not "click here". The word that says what is on the other side.
hint4.click-here: Make the link out of the words that describe the destination. They are right there in the sentence: "this one" or "the same model".
why.naked-url: The address still shows on the page. It may click, but it is bare — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bicycle. A blind person hears that read out, character by character. The address is for the machine. The word is for the person.
hint2.naked-url: The address belongs in href, not between the tags.
hint3.naked-url: a short word — the ugly part is hidden, the person sees the word.
hint4.naked-url: Take the address out of the sentence. It goes into href. A word from the text takes its place.
why.lost-sentence: The sentence is gone. The task is to make a link INSIDE it, not to replace it.
hint2.lost-sentence: "It is the same model as this one" must stay. You are only making part of it clickable.
why.lost-h1: The heading is gone. The task is the link, not deleting things.
blocks.0.text: You want to show someone the model of your bike. You wrote the address straight into the sentence. Run it.
blocks.1.text: It comes out as text. It does not click, it is not blue, it does nothing. To the browser these are just letters — it cannot guess you meant them as a link.
blocks.4.text: <a> is the tag. The href attribute says where it leads. And what sits between the opening and closing tag is the word you click on.
blocks.5.text: Notice the split: the address is for the machine, the word is for the person. So there is no need to show the address — it can be ugly and long.
blocks.7.text: Here is where almost everyone goes wrong. The temptation is to write "click here" or just "here".
blocks.8.text: Do not. Take the word out of the sentence and read it alone: "here". Where? A blind person often skims ONLY the links on a page — their computer pulls them into a list. If they all say "here", they know where none of them lead.
blocks.9.text: The rule is simple: the word must say what is on the other side, even when read outside its sentence.
blocks.10.text: "Click here" is a link that only works for someone looking at the screen.
blocks.12.text: Add target="_blank" and the link opens in a new tab. Useful when you send someone out and do not want them to leave your page.
blocks.13.text: But do not put it everywhere. You are deciding for someone what to do with their own browser — and they may not want a new tab.
blocks.14.text: The general rule: links out — new tab. Links inside your own site — same tab.
blocks.15.text: Your turn. Make the address into a link — and mind which word you choose.
